text:
The past few years have seen a proliferation of digital technologies such as mobile money, social media, crisis mapping and big data analytics, transforming the environment in which humanitarians work. While there are many positive accounts about how technology will solve humanitarian problems, most are published in online media and therefore carry little weight in the academy. As a result there is a need for scholarly work that critically examines these developments and provides an understanding of what technologies mean for humanitarians and for the humanitarian enterprise as a whole. In short we need more rigorous research into the humanitarian technology revolution and how it is changing and will continue to change humanitarian action – and this is what our research agenda aims to achieve.

As co-editors of the special issue of Humanity, this article is the authors’ reflection on what prisms to apply when examining the humanitarian consequences of technological innovations in the context of increasingly complex emergencies. They propose five focal points or prisms of analysis: the transformation of humanitarian space, the new forms of public-private partnership that technology has enabled, the distribution of digital food and other new forms of aid, the new configurations of relationships between aid providers and recipients, which are shaped by technologies of remote management, and the creation of new sources of human vulnerability through data collection practices. The authors argue that technology is always political; it carries the interests of various professionals and actors who deploy it with their own projects and sometimes with inadequate regard for humanitarian values. The article is based on STS analysis and aims to reflect on the conditions under which humanitarian actors are able to maintain their fundamental principles of the “do no harm” principle of humanity, neutrality and impartiality in the digital age and to encourage more reflexive practices that take account of the power relations, ethical choices and digital inequalities that are associated with technological developments.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
